results.possibleIdentity: James Loton Flanagan (1925–2015) was a distinguished American electrical engineer and researcher at AT&T Bell Laboratories, where he was active from 1957 until 1990. Based in Murray Hill, New Jersey, he served as the head of the Speech and Auditory Research Department starting in 1961 and became the head of the Acoustics Research Department in 1967. His technical work focused on the physiology of speech and hearing, including modeling the basilar membrane of the inner ear and the vocal tract for speech production. This research formed the basis for his seminal textbook, 'Speech Analysis, Synthesis and Perception,' first published in 1965 and revised in 1972. Flanagan's work was foundational to modern speech technologies such as digital voice coding, speech recognition, and synthesis. He was a pioneer in using digital computers for acoustic signal processing and held over 50 patents, including one for the design of the modern artificial larynx. Given his leadership position in acoustics at Bell Labs during the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was a key figure in the environment where the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was founded and operated by his colleagues Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer.
